title: Generalized flux-weighted boundary walls in kinetic models
abstract: We present a technique to investigate the stationary states of a system of a collisionless system confined by an external potential and coupled to boundary reservoirs through prescribed reinjection rules. We consider a family of boundary conditions parametrized by an integer $n$, corresponding to different velocity distributions imposed at the boundaries, generalizing the standard flux-weighted Maxwellian scheme. By combining Liouville's theorem with the boundary injection rule, we derive an explicit analytical expression for the stationary distribution function. This framework provides a direct link between microscopic boundary dynamics and macroscopic stationary profiles. We show that thermal equilibrium is recovered only for the standard flux-weighted injection method, while for all other cases the system relaxes to manifestly non-thermal stationary states. The resulting density and temperature profiles exhibit non-trivial spatial structures, including non-monotonic behaviour and temperature gradients induced by the boundary conditions alone. Analytical predictions for stationary moments are obtained in closed form for representative cases and are nicely reproduced by particle-based numerical simulations.
summary: This paper presents a new technique to study the stationary states of collisionless systems connected to boundary reservoirs. By generalizing the boundary injection rules using a parameter n, the authors show that only one specific rule leads to standard thermal equilibrium. Other injection rules produce non-thermal stationary states characterized by unexpected spatial structures like temperature gradients and non-monotonic density profiles. The analytical results are confirmed through particle-based numerical simulations.
main_problem: The lack of a general theoretical framework to predict the stationary distribution function and macroscopic profiles of collisionless systems coupled to boundary reservoirs with various injection rules.
main_result: The authors derive an analytical expression for the stationary distribution function, proving that thermal equilibrium is only recovered for standard flux-weighted Maxwellian injection (n=1), while other rules lead to non-thermal states with non-trivial spatial structures.
method: The study combines Jeans' theorem (Liouville's theorem) with boundary injection rules to derive analytical solutions, which are validated using N-particle numerical simulations with a fourth-order symplectic integrator.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional, collisionless, non-interacting system of particles confined within a finite domain by an external potential U(q), where particles are reinjected at the boundaries according to a family of distributions parameterized by an integer n.
key_observables: Stationary distribution function f(q, p), number density profiles n(q), kinetic temperature profiles T(q), and stationary moments.
important_parameters: The integer parameter n (defining the injection rule), temperature T, and the external potential U(q).
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be collisionless, non-interacting, and one-dimensional.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the time evolution of kinetic energy and stationary density/temperature profiles for n=0; Figure 2 shows the thermal equilibrium case for n=1; Figure 3 shows non-monotonic density and decreasing temperature profiles for n=3.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of boundary-driven stationary states, defines the generalized injection model, provides the analytical derivation of the distribution function using Jeans' theorem, presents numerical validation for specific cases (n=0, 1, 3), and discusses the broader implications for kinetic models.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a rigorous framework for understanding how microscopic boundary driving/injection rules directly dictate the emergence of non-equilibrium steady states and non-thermal macroscopic profiles.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Left panel: time evolution of the total kinetic energy, computed from Eq. (49), showing the approach to the stationary state. Right panel: stationary density profile (red) and kinetic temperature profile (green) as functions of q ∈[−π, π], obtained from numerical simulations of Eq. (1) with the potential U(q) given by Eq. (28). The boundary condition corresponds to the case n = 0, implemented via Eq. (6). The corresponding analytical predictions, computed from Eq. (32) for the density and Eq. (33) for the temperature are marked by the heavy black lines. Simulation parameters: T = 3, N = 216, and dt = 10−2, xth ≃−0.30, ε = 10−12,Nmax = 50.

Figure 2: Same quantities as in Fig. 1, with the same colour coding and parameters. In this case, the boundary condition corresponds to mass flux (n = 1), implemented via Eq. (7). The analytical density and temperature profiles are computed from Eqs. (46).

Figure 3: Same quantities as in the previous figures, with the same colour coding and parameters. In this case, the boundary condition corresponds to energy flux (n = 3), implemented via Eq. (8). The analytical density and temperature profiles are computed from Eqs. (47) and (48), respectively.
